topic: natural wine opinions — loves some, hates others

turns.content: ugh can we talk about natural wine for a second because I just had an argument with our sommelier that almost ended in someone throwing a glass
Oh no — what happened? Natural wine seems to spark strong feelings in everyone who works in food. What was the argument about?

ok so he wants to put this orange wine on the pairing for my new duck dish. an ORANGE WINE with duck. I tasted it and it's like someone fermented a dirty sock in a barrel of kombucha. it has this funky, vinegary thing happening that completely murders the sauce. and he's standing there telling me I don't have a sophisticated enough palate to appreciate it

Ha — "not sophisticated enough" is a bold thing to say to a sous chef about a pairing with their own dish. Do you think there's any version of an orange wine that could work, or is the whole category wrong for what you're doing with the duck?

ok look, I'm not anti-natural wine. some of them are genuinely beautiful. I had a Domaine Ganevat Chardonnay last year that made me want to cry it was so good, and that's natural. there's a winemaker in the Jura, I forget her name, who does these skin-contact whites that are just... parfait. elegant. restrained. but this thing he wants to pair? it tastes like a science experiment gone wrong. not every natural wine is good just because it's natural, tabarnac

That's a really fair distinction — loving the philosophy of natural winemaking doesn't mean you have to accept every bottle that comes out of it. The Ganevat wines are genuinely exceptional. It sounds like your issue isn't with the category, it's with this specific bottle and the way it interacts with your sauce. What's the sauce like?

it's a cherry reduction with black pepper and a hint of star anise. delicate. the flavours need something that supports them, not something that screams over them. a good burgundy would be perfect. or even a lighter natural red with some structure. but this orange wine is so aggressive on the palate it just... blyat, it erases everything I built in that sauce

A cherry reduction with star anise needs something that can harmonize with those warm spice notes without competing — I can see why a volatile orange wine would bulldoze that. A lighter Pinot or even a cool-climate Gamay could thread that needle. Have you suggested alternatives, or is the sommelier dug in?

he's dug in like a mule. he keeps saying the guests want "discovery" and "conversation starters" and I keep saying the guests want food that tastes good with wine that tastes good TOGETHER. that's the whole point of a pairing, non? you don't pair for shock value. you pair because the combination creates something neither one does alone

You're describing the fundamental purpose of a pairing perfectly — it should be greater than the sum of its parts, not a clash that makes people notice the wine instead of the food. The "discovery" angle can still work if you find a natural wine that's interesting AND complementary. Is this something the head chef would weigh in on, or is it between you two?

Marc-Andre doesn't care about pairings honestly. he's all about the numbers. as long as the wine has a decent margin he's happy. so it's between me and the sommelier, and I am NOT losing this fight. I'll bring in three alternatives tomorrow and do a blind tasting with the front of house. if his funky sock wine wins, fine, I'll shut up. but it won't. I know my sauce

A blind tasting is the perfect move — it takes the ego out of it and lets the pairing speak for itself. If your sauce is as precise as it sounds, the right wine will be obvious once people taste them side by side. Bring your best contenders and let the palate decide. I'd bet on the cherry reduction winning that argument.
oh it will. the sauce always wins. ok I need to go research some bottles. if you hear about a sous chef strangling a sommelier in Montreal tomorrow, you know nothing. bonne nuit
Ha — my lips are sealed. Go find those bottles and crush that blind tasting tomorrow. The sauce deserves a partner, not an opponent. Bonne nuit, and try not to plot any crimes before you sleep.
